edges.description: Retention hyperkeratosis obstructs follicles and seeds microcomedone development.
Visible comedones arise as microcomedones enlarge into obstructed follicular lesions.
C. acnes colonization activates innate immune programs that include inflammasome signaling.
Inflammasome activation amplifies inflammatory cytokine signaling in acne lesions.
Cytokine-driven follicular inflammation produces erythematous inflammatory papules.
Inflammatory cytokines recruit neutrophils and produce pustular acne lesions.
Severe deep inflammation and follicular rupture produce nodular and cystic lesions.
Acne inflammation can leave post-inflammatory dyspigmentation after lesion resolution.
Deep inflammatory acne lesions can heal through dermal remodeling and atrophic scarring.

nodes.description: Targeted laser therapy that selectively affects sebaceous glands and is being evaluated for moderate to severe acne.

evidence.reference_title: 1726 nm Lasers for the Treatment of Acne Vulgaris.
evidence.snippet: New 1726 nm lasers specifically target the sebaceous gland, which is known to be pivotal in acne pathophysiology.

nodes.description: Azelaic acid peels reduce sebum and lesion burden as a nonpharmacologic option for facial acne.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy of 30% azelaic acid peel in the nonpharmacological treatment of facial acne.
evidence.snippet: Peels with 30% AZA reduced acne lesions and normalized the activity of the sebaceous glands.

nodes.description: Antimicrobial agent that kills C. acnes through oxidative damage without promoting antibiotic resistance. Also has mild comedolytic activity.

evidence.reference_title: Benzoyl peroxide: a review of its current use in the treatment of acne vulgaris.
evidence.snippet: BPO-containing combinations do not induce bacterial resistance and are important first-line treatments for mild to moderate acne vulgaris.

nodes.description: Blue light phototherapy is used as an alternative for inflammatory acne, with evidence of clinical improvement.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of Blue Light on Acne Vulgaris: A Systematic Review.
evidence.snippet: Studies have shown significant improvements in the overall picture of acne.

nodes.description: Cutibacterium acnes expands within occluded follicles and contributes to lesion progression from noninflammatory comedones to inflamed papules.

evidence.reference_title: The role of inflammation in the pathology of acne.
evidence.snippet: Propionibacterium acnes colonizes the duct of the sebaceous follicle, causing an innate immune response and the progression from a so-called noninflammatory comedo to an inflammatory papule, pustule, or nodule.

nodes.description: Triple-combination topical therapy for moderate to severe acne, combining clindamycin phosphate, adapalene, and benzoyl peroxide for long-term use.

evidence.reference_title: Long-term efficacy and tolerability of clindamycin phosphate 1.2%/adapalene 0.15%/benzoyl peroxide 3.1% gel for acne: pooled results from two 6-month studies.
evidence.snippet: At week 24, 67% of participants achieved treatment success, and significant reductions from baseline in inflammatory (88%) and noninflammatory (68%) lesions were observed (p < 0.001, both).

nodes.description: Non-inflammatory lesions including open comedones (blackheads) and closed comedones (whiteheads) resulting from follicular plugging.

evidence.reference_title: The role of inflammation in the pathology of acne.
evidence.snippet: evidence has emerged supporting a role for inflammation at all stages of acne lesion development, perhaps subclinically even before comedo formation

nodes.description: Inflammasome and innate immune activation drive cytokine release that recruits neutrophils and sustains inflammatory lesion formation.

evidence.snippet: The inflammasomes are intracellular multimeric protein complexes consisting of an innate immune sensor, the adapter protein ASC and the inflammatory caspases-1 and/or -11 and are important for the host defense against pathogens.

nodes.description: Dysregulated keratinocyte differentiation and desquamation within the follicular epithelium promotes retention hyperkeratosis.

evidence.reference_title: Effects of Diet on Acne and Its Response to Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Acne pathogenesis is attributed to four key factors: excess sebum production, hyperproliferation of Cutibacterium acnes (C. acnes, formerly called Propionibacterium acnes) bacteria, hyperkeratinization of the pilosebaceous follicles, and inflammatory mechanisms

nodes.description: Combined oral contraceptives and spironolactone reduce androgen effects on sebaceous glands. Used in female patients with hormonal acne patterns.

evidence.reference_title: A Review of hormone-based therapies to treat adult acne vulgaris in women.
evidence.snippet: Hormone-based therapies including combined oral contraceptive medications and spironolactone are considered effective therapies to treat adult acne in women.

nodes.description: Innate immune sensing activates inflammasome pathways that amplify inflammatory signaling in acne lesions.

evidence.snippet: The inflammasomes are intracellular multimeric protein complexes consisting of an innate immune sensor, the adapter protein ASC and the inflammatory caspases-1 and/or -11 and are important for the host defense against pathogens.

nodes.description: Small, raised, erythematous lesions resulting from inflammation around occluded follicles.

evidence.reference_title: The role of inflammation in the pathology of acne.
evidence.snippet: the progression from a so-called noninflammatory comedo to an inflammatory papule, pustule, or nodule

nodes.description: Follicular obstruction from retained keratin and sebum leads to early microcomedone development that seeds later visible comedones.

evidence.reference_title: Effects of Diet on Acne and Its Response to Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Acne pathogenesis is attributed to four key factors: excess sebum production, hyperproliferation of Cutibacterium acnes (C. acnes, formerly called Propionibacterium acnes) bacteria, hyperkeratinization of the pilosebaceous follicles, and inflammatory mechanisms

nodes.description: Deep, painful lesions representing severe inflammatory acne with potential for scarring.

evidence.reference_title: Therapeutic considerations for severe nodular acne.
evidence.snippet: Severe nodular acne, defined as grade 4 or 5 acne on the Investigator's Static Global Assessment scale, is a skin condition characterized by intense erythema, inflammation, nodules, cysts, and scarring.

nodes.description: Tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline) are used for moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne. Have both antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, but prolonged use contributes to antimicrobial resistance and requires stewardship.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and adverse events of oral isotretinoin for acne: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: isotretinoin therapy reduced acne lesion counts by a clinically relevant amount, and always by a greater amount than control, which was either placebo (two studies), oral antibiotics (seven studies) or other control

evidence.reference_title: Antibiotics and Antimicrobial Resistance in Acne: Epidemiological Trends and Clinical Practice Considerations.
evidence.snippet: The overuse of topical and/or systemic antibiotics, the long treatment courses used for acne, and the availability of over-the-counter antibiotic preparations, have led to the worldwide emergence of resistant strains in acne patients.

nodes.description: Systemic retinoid reserved for severe, recalcitrant acne. Addresses all four pathogenic factors but requires careful monitoring due to teratogenicity and other adverse effects.

evidence.reference_title: Systematic review of low-dose isotretinoin for treatment of acne vulgaris: Focus on indication, dosage, regimen, efficacy, safety, satisfaction, and follow up, based on clinical studies.
evidence.snippet: Oral isotretinoin is the most effective treatment for moderate to severe acne and its side effects are mostly dose-dependent.

nodes.description: Photodynamic therapy is used off-label for inflammatory acne with evidence supporting efficacy in dermatologic indications.

evidence.reference_title: Photodynamic therapy in dermatology: established and new indications.
evidence.snippet: Newer indications, for which no approval has yet been granted, but which nevertheless have sufficient evidence of efficacy according to the study situation, are inflammatory (lichen sclerosus, acne) and infectious dermatoses (viral warts, cutaneous leishmaniasis, atypical mycobacteriosis).

nodes.description: Darkening of skin at sites of resolved acne lesions, particularly common in individuals with darker skin tones. This sequela results from inflammatory damage to melanocytes and is a common concern in patients with skin of color.

evidence.reference_title: The Pathogenesis and Management of Acne-Induced Post-inflammatory Hyperpigmentation.
evidence.snippet: the inflammatory processes of acne stimulate excess melanogenesis and abnormal melanin deposition, leading to pigmentary sequelae known as post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation

nodes.description: Inflamed lesions containing purulent material, representing more advanced inflammatory response.

evidence.reference_title: Pustular skin disorders: diagnosis and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Localized pustular eruptions are seen on the hands and feet in adults with pustulosis palmaris et plantaris and acrodermatitis continua (both of which may be variants of psoriasis); on the face in patients with acne vulgaris, rosacea, and perioral dermatitis

nodes.description: Permanent textural changes including atrophic (ice pick, boxcar, rolling) and hypertrophic scars resulting from severe or inadequately treated acne.

evidence.reference_title: Acne Scarring-Pathogenesis, Evaluation, and Treatment Options.
evidence.snippet: This review focuses on atrophic scars, the most common type of acne scar.

nodes.description: Androgen and IGF-1 signaling increase sebocyte activity, elevating sebum output and creating a lipid-rich follicular environment.

evidence.reference_title: Effects of Diet on Acne and Its Response to Treatment.
evidence.snippet: In acne, mTORC1 mediates sebaceous gland hyperproliferation, lipid synthesis, and hyperplasia of keratinocytes

nodes.description: Clindamycin and erythromycin reduce C. acnes populations and have anti-inflammatory effects. Should be combined with benzoyl peroxide to prevent resistance.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of acne with topical antibiotics: lessons from clinical studies.
evidence.snippet: topical antibiotics remain one of the cornerstones of acne management

nodes.description: First-line treatment that normalizes follicular keratinization, reduces comedone formation, and has anti-inflammatory effects. Examples include tretinoin, adapalene, and tazarotene.

evidence.reference_title: Topical Retinoids in Acne Vulgaris: A Systematic Review.
evidence.snippet: Topical retinoids are safe and efficacious for the treatment of acne vulgaris.

nodes.description: Topical retinoid selective for retinoic acid receptor gamma with evidence for reducing comedonal and inflammatory lesions, including truncal acne.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Acne Vulgaris With Trifarotene.
evidence.snippet: Large-scale randomized, controlled clinical trials have demonstrated trifarotene to be safe, well tolerated, and efficacious in reducing both comedones and papules/pustules of acne.
